votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the absolute value of g equals the greatest common divisor of a and b.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the absolute value of g equals the greatest common divisor of a and b using math.gcd.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the absolute value of g equals the greatest common divisor of a and b.

The Python code correctly checks whether abs(g) equals gcd(a, b). Python's math.gcd returns the same value as Coq's Z.gcd for integers (both return the non-negative gcd), and Python's abs matches Coq's Z.abs. The spec is correctly translated as a boolean predicate checking the same condition.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. Both check if abs(g) equals gcd(a, b). Python's math.gcd returns the same value as Coq's Z.gcd for integers (both return non-negative gcd). The only subtlety is that math.gcd always returns non-negative values, matching Z.gcd's behavior, and abs() in Python matches Z.abs in Coq for integers.

The Python code correctly checks if abs(g) equals gcd(a, b). Python's math.gcd returns the same value as Coq's Z.gcd for integers (both return the non-negative gcd). The abs function in Python matches Z.abs in Coq. The spec checks the same condition on concrete inputs.
Both check that abs(g) equals the gcd of a and b; Python’s math.gcd matches Coq’s Z.gcd for all integers (nonnegative result, gcd(0,0)=0).
Both check abs(g) equals the nonnegative gcd of a and b; Python’s math.gcd matches Coq’s Z.gcd behavior (including negatives and 0).
Both compare abs(g) to the nonnegative gcd of a and b. Python’s math.gcd matches Coq’s Z.gcd semantics (including negatives and zeros), so results coincide for all inputs.
